title: Psalms 78
subtitle: Give Ear, O My People, to My Teaching

sections.header: Verses 1–39 — “The Things Our Ancestors Have Told Us”

heb.paragraph: "I will open my mouth with a parable (māshāl); I will utter hidden things, things from of old" (v.2). Jesus quotes this in Matthew 13:35. The longest historical psalm (72 verses). Asaph retells Israel’s history as a cautionary tale: the exodus, the wilderness rebellions, the manna and quail, the plagues on Egypt. The purpose: "so the next generation would know" (v.6). History is pedagogy. The first half traces God’s faithfulness against Israel’s stubbornness: they tested God in the wilderness (v.18), forgot his deeds (v.11), turned back like a faulty bow (v.57).

notes.note: "We will tell the next generation the praiseworthy deeds of the LORD. He decreed statutes for Jacob... which he commanded our ancestors to teach their children." Intergenerational faith transmission: the previous generation’s story becomes the next generation’s foundation.

"Then they would put their trust in God and not forget his deeds. They would not be like their ancestors — a stubborn and rebellious generation." The purpose of history: produce trust, prevent repetition.

"They spoke against God: ‘Can God really spread a table in the wilderness?’ He rained down manna; he gave them the grain of heaven." The test: can God provide? The answer: manna. Human doubt, divine abundance.

refs.note: Matthew quotes verse 2 ('I will utter things hidden since creation') as fulfilled in Jesus's parables.
Paul's rehearsal of Israel's wilderness failures draws on the same history this psalm recounts.
The warning against hardening hearts uses the same exodus generation as cautionary example.

hist.context: Psalm 78 is the second-longest psalm (72 verses), a historical retrospective rehearsing Israel's story from Egypt to David. The opening frames it as wisdom instruction passed between generations (vv.1-8). The label 'parable' (māshāl) and 'hidden things' (ḥîdôt) in verse 2 indicates this is not mere chronicle but interpreted history with didactic purpose.

heb.paragraph: "He chose David (Dāwîd) his servant and took him from the sheep pens... to be the shepherd of his people Jacob" (vv.70–71). The psalm recounts Egypt’s plagues (vv.44–51), the wilderness wandering (vv.52–55), Israel’s continued rebellion even in the promised land (vv.56–58), and God’s judgment: Shiloh rejected, the ark captured (vv.60–64). Then the turn: God awoke as from sleep (v.65), chose Judah and Mount Zion (vv.68–69), and selected David the shepherd to be king (vv.70–72). History moves toward David.

notes.note: "How often they rebelled against him in the wilderness. Again and again they put God to the test. They did not remember his power." Repetitive rebellion: the pattern never breaks on its own. Human nature repeats; grace must interrupt.

"He abandoned the tabernacle of Shiloh, the tent he had set up among humans. He sent the ark of his might into captivity." God withdraws from Shiloh — the most shocking event in Israel’s pre-monarchic history.

"He chose David his servant and took him from the sheep pens; from tending the sheep he brought him to be the shepherd of his people. And David shepherded them with integrity of heart; with skilful hands he led them." The psalm’s climax: from sheep pens to throne. David the shepherd shepherds God’s people. Integrity and skill: character and competence.

refs.note: Jesus's bread of life discourse interprets the manna this psalm describes as pointing to himself.
Stephen's speech recounts the same pattern of rebellion and idolatry the psalm laments.
The transition from Saul to David that the psalm implies is explicitly narrated by Paul.

hist.context: The psalm traces cycles of divine provision, human rebellion, and divine judgment. The wilderness generation 'put God to the test' (v.18), yet God provided manna ('bread of angels,' v.25) and quail. The climax rejects Ephraim/Shiloh (where the ark was captured) in favor of Judah/Zion and David. This Judean perspective shapes the telling.

ppl.name: Jacob
ppl.text: Jacob is a key figure in Give Ear, O My People, to My Teaching.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVMy people, hear my teaching; listen to the words of my mouth.ESVGive ear, O my people, to my teaching; incline your ears to the words of my mouth!NIVThey would not be like their ancestors— a stubborn and rebellious generation, whose hearts were not loyal to God, whose spirits were not faithful to him.ESVand that they should not be like their fathers, a stubborn and rebellious generation, a generation whose heart was not steadfast, whose spirit was not faithful to God.

rec.text: ""We will tell the next generation the praiseworthy deeds of the LORD. He decreed statutes for Jacob... which he commanded our ancestors to teach their children." Intergenerational faith transmission: …"

rows.label: David Chosen — 78:40–72
rows.text: Plagues. Shiloh abandoned. Then: "He chose David from the sheep pens." Shepherd to king.

hebtext.note: "I will open my mouth with a parable (māshāl); I will utter hidden things, things from of old" (v.2). Jesus quotes this in Matthew 13:35. The longest historical psalm (72 verses). Asaph retells Israel’s history as a cautionary tale: the exodus, the wilderness rebellions, the manna and quail, the plagues on Egypt. The purpose: "so the next generation would know" (v.6). History is pedagogy. The first half traces God’s faithfulness against Israel’s stubbornness: they tested God in the wilderness (v.18), forgot his deeds (v.11), turned back like a faulty bow (v.57).

"He chose David (Dāwîd) his servant and took him from the sheep pens... to be the shepherd of his people Jacob" (vv.70–71). The psalm recounts Egypt’s plagues (vv.44–51), the wilderness wandering (vv.52–55), Israel’s continued rebellion even in the promised land (vv.56–58), and God’s judgment: Shiloh rejected, the ark captured (vv.60–64). Then the turn: God awoke as from sleep (v.65), chose Judah and Mount Zion (vv.68–69), and selected David the shepherd to be king (vv.70–72). History moves toward David.

themes.note: Covenant peaks: history as covenant-memory transmission. Prophecy: Jesus quotes v.2 (Matt 13:35); David’s selection from sheep pens. Judgment: rebellion’s consequences traced.
